questions.scenario: You need to print a startup sequence for three reactor systems: alpha, beta, gamma.
questions.question: Use a for-in loop to print 'Starting: SYSTEM' for alpha, beta, and gamma.
questions.description: for item in list; do ... done iterates over space-separated words. The list can be literal words, a glob expansion, command substitution, or an array. Each item is assigned to the loop variable in turn.
use_cases: Process a fixed list of servers
Run a command for each item in a config list
hints: for item in word1 word2 word3; do ... done
Use echo inside with the loop variable
Run: for sys in alpha beta gamma; do echo "Starting: $sys"; done

validation.command: for sys in alpha beta gamma; do echo "Starting: $sys"; done

questions.scenario: A script needs to retry a command up to 5 times. Use a C-style loop with a counter.
questions.question: Use a C-style for loop to print 'Attempt N' for N from 1 to 5.
questions.description: for (( init; condition; step )) is the C-style for loop. Supports the full set of arithmetic operators. Useful when you need a numeric counter with specific start/end/step values.
use_cases: Retry loops with a max attempt count
hints: Syntax: for (( i=1; i<=5; i++ )); do ... done
Run: for (( i=1; i<=5; i++ )); do echo "Attempt $i"; done

validation.command: for (( i=1; i<=5; i++ )); do echo "Attempt $i"; done

questions.scenario: You have a file of server names, one per line. Process each one in a loop.
questions.question: Loop over /home/student/data/processes.txt line by line using while read and print each line.
questions.description: while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file is the canonical way to read a file line by line. IFS= prevents trimming leading/trailing whitespace. -r prevents backslash interpretation. The < redirects the file into the while loop.
use_cases: Process log files line by line
Apply a command to each line of output
hints: while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file
The < feeds the file to the while loop
Run: while IFS= read -r line; do echo "$line"; done < /home/student/data/processes.txt

validation.command: while IFS= read -r line; do echo "$line"; done < /home/student/data/processes.txt

questions.scenario: A loop searches for 'postgres' in a list and must stop as soon as it's found.
questions.question: Loop over the words 'nginx postgres backup' and break when you find 'postgres'.
questions.description: break exits the innermost loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next. Both work in for, while, and until loops. break N exits N levels of nested loops.
use_cases: Stop searching as soon as a match is found
Skip malformed entries in a loop
Exit a retry loop on success
hints: Use break inside the loop when the condition matches
for item in ... with an if inside
Run: for svc in nginx postgres backup; do [[ "$svc" == 'postgres' ]] && { echo "Found: $svc"; break; }; done

validation.command: for svc in nginx postgres backup; do [[ "$svc" == 'postgres' ]] && { echo "Found: $svc"; break; }; done

questions.scenario: A script monitors a condition and should keep running until it's met (not while it's met).
questions.question: Use an until loop that increments a counter from 0 until it reaches 3, printing each value.
questions.description: until condition; do ... done is the opposite of while — it loops UNTIL the condition becomes true (while it's false). Useful when the natural phrasing is 'keep going until X happens' rather than 'keep going while X holds'.
use_cases: Wait until a service port is open
hints: Increment a counter and check when it hits 3
Run: N=0; until (( N >= 3 )); do echo $N; (( N++ )); done
